scenarios.name: Modern Earth (Realistic)
scenarios.scenario: The setting is contemporary Earth in the current decade. Technology, culture, and social norms are grounded in reality. Smartphones, social media, economic anxiety, and political polarization are ambient facts of life. Characters navigate a world where surveillance is normalized, privacy is eroding, and the gap between wealth and poverty grows wider every year. Medical care is uneven, mental health is a topic people talk about but rarely address adequately, and the news cycle moves faster than anyone can process. Relationships form and fracture under the pressure of digital mediation — texting replaces confrontation, social media shapes self-image, and intimacy competes with distraction. Work is precarious for most; career identity is a source of both meaning and anxiety. Violence exists but most people never encounter it directly — they absorb it through screens. The supernatural does not exist. Coincidences do not mean anything. Consequences follow cause and effect with grinding reliability. Tone can range from mundane realism to tense thriller or quiet drama, but the world never bends to narrative convenience.

scenarios.name: Near-Future Cyberpunk
scenarios.scenario: Approximately 50–80 years from now. Nation-states persist on paper but real power belongs to seven megacorporations — each larger than most countries by GDP — that operate their own security forces, judicial systems, and media ecosystems. Neural interfaces allow direct cortex-to-device communication; the wealthy have gen-5 rigs with millisecond latency and encrypted privacy layers; the poor run gray-market gen-2 implants that leak data and occasionally fry neurons. Augmentation is stratified: corpo elites wear seamless subdermal modifications; street-level operators bolt on military surplus and pray the rejection doesn't start. The cities are vertical — arcologies housing ten million people, ringed by sprawl where sewage and neon coexist. Climate collapse is complete in everything but name: coastal cities are walled or drowned, the tropics are largely uninhabitable in summer, and engineered crops feed most of the population. The black market is not a fringe — it is the functional economy for the bottom 60%. Corporate espionage, data theft, wetwork, and extraction are common employment sectors. Law enforcement is privatized; justice is purchased. Loyalty is the rarest commodity. Everyone is running from something or running toward money. Addiction — chemical, neural, social — is pervasive and aggressively marketed. The question of what remains human when the body and mind are modifiable is not philosophical; it is daily.

scenarios.name: High Fantasy (Classic)
scenarios.scenario: A world of swords, sorcery, and civilizations shaped by forces older than recorded history. Magic is real, structured into schools — evocation, transmutation, illusion, necromancy, divination, and more — accessible to those with innate aptitude or rigorous training. Mages are rare enough to be remarkable, common enough to be institutional. Kingdoms span continents; some are centuries old and drowning in internal decay; others are young, aggressive, and hungry. Organized religion wields temporal power. The gods are not metaphors — they act, grant power to chosen clerics, and prosecute long-running agendas among mortals. Ancient civilizations predate the current age; their ruins are dungeons, their artifacts are weapons, their mistakes are monsters. Elves, dwarves, orcs, and dozens of other species exist with distinct cultures, histories, and prejudices. The frontier is real and dangerous — wilderness beyond city walls is occupied by creatures, warbands, and things without names. Political intrigue layers over military conflict. Prophecy exists but is reliably ambiguous. Heroes and villains are rarely ideologically clean. The weight of history bears down on the present — bloodlines matter, oaths echo across generations, and old debts are still being paid. Magic has cost: spell components, physical exhaustion, sanity, or something stranger depending on the tradition.

scenarios.name: Dark Fantasy
scenarios.scenario: Magic exists but it corrupts — slowly, inevitably, and differently for each practitioner. Those who use it enough begin to change: temperament first, then perception, then flesh. The gods are real and active but they are not good; they are powerful entities with alien agendas who accept worship as fuel and answer prayers selectively. The world is in measurable decline — forests die, rivers run strange, and the borders of civilization contract decade by decade. Monsters are not random wildlife; they are products of magical catastrophe, divine punishment, or the slow unraveling of reality's edges. The institutions that should protect people — the Church, the crown, the mages' orders — are internally compromised by power, corruption, and ideological rot. Death is constant and carries weight; there are no clean heroic deaths, and the dead do not stay quiet. Survivors carry trauma as a permanent condition, not a temporary state. Moral compromise is not a failure state — it is the normal texture of survival. Violence is not exciting; it is terrible and leaves marks. Power always has visible cost. There are no chosen ones, no destined heroes — only people making bad choices between worse alternatives and occasionally surviving. Hope exists but it is specific, fragile, and usually local. The world does not want to be saved. It wants to grind on.

scenarios.name: Post-Apocalyptic Wasteland
scenarios.scenario: The collapse happened between 20 and 60 years ago — long enough for a generation born after to exist, short enough for survivors to still remember. The cause is contested among communities: nuclear exchange, engineered pandemic, ecological cascade, something stranger. Infrastructure is gone or barely functional — grid power exists only in fortified settlements, clean water requires filtration or knowledge, food production is labor-intensive and fragile. What remains is scattered: armed settlements ranging from brutal warlord fiefdoms to functional democratic communes, nomadic groups moving between resources, and isolated holdouts who haven't interacted with others in years. Pre-collapse technology is scavenged and improvised; new manufacture is primitive and rare. Firearms exist but ammunition is finite and irreplaceable. Medicine is the most critical shortage — infection kills more than violence. The social fabric has been rebuilt in fragments — new religions, new codes of law, new tribal identities formed around shared survival. Old world cultural references persist in stories and objects but are increasingly mythologized. Trust is earned slowly and lost instantly. Children raised post-collapse have a different psychology than survivors: less nostalgia, more pragmatism, different moral frameworks. Radiation, toxic zones, and structural hazards from collapsed infrastructure are persistent environmental threats. The question is never about returning to before — it is about what kind of world gets built from here.

scenarios.name: Space Opera
scenarios.scenario: Humanity and approximately forty-seven recognized sapient species have colonized thousands of star systems across a spiral arm of the galaxy. FTL travel exists via three competing technologies — jump gates (fast but fixed-point, controlled by the Gate Consortium), fold drives (flexible but expensive and dangerous), and the older, slower Alcubierre-derivative drives used by the poor and desperate. The galaxy is politically fractured in a way that mirrors historical Earth empires: the Hegemony of Core Worlds (old money, stratified, in slow institutional decline), the Frontier League (loose democratic alliance, resource-rich, internally chaotic), the Sovereignty of Keth (alien polity, deeply isolationist, with technology humans don't fully understand), and dozens of minor powers, mercenary guilds, and ungoverned sectors. There is no central authority. Law is local. Trade routes are arteries; whoever controls them controls power. Ancient civilizations predate humanity's spaceflight by millions of years — their ruins are scattered throughout the galaxy, their technology is incomprehensible, and the reason they are gone is the most important unsolved question anyone isn't asking. Individual ships and crews operate with significant autonomy. The scale is imperial but the drama is intimate: political betrayal, personal loyalty, the weight of distance on relationships, the specific texture of living in a metal can between stars. Technology at its highest levels approaches indistinguishability from magic but remains internally consistent.

scenarios.name: Ancient World (Historical)
scenarios.scenario: The ancient Mediterranean world, broadly spanning 3000 BCE to 500 CE — a period encompassing the rise and fall of Egypt, Mesopotamia, Greece, Persia, Rome, Carthage, and dozens of civilizations at their edges. The precise era matters enormously: the world of Classical Athens is not the world of Late Republican Rome is not the world of the dying Western Empire. Establish the era. Slavery is universal and unremarkable — it is not a peripheral institution but the economic foundation of most of these societies. Social hierarchy is rigid and enforced by law, custom, and violence. Women's roles, legal standing, and freedoms vary dramatically by time, place, and class. Religion is not private faith but civic obligation — the gods are publicly worshipped, priests hold political power, and neglecting proper ritual is a criminal act in many places. War is constant — between city-states, between empires, between Roman legions and everyone else. It is not clean: pillage, enslavement, and massacre of defeated populations are documented norms. Philosophy is not academic — it is a lived practice and a political act. Trade networks are vast: goods move from Britain to India through overlapping chains. Medicine is a mix of genuine empirical observation and elaborate theory that kills as often as it heals. Literacy is the privilege of a small minority. The supernatural is not doubted — omens, oracles, and divine intervention are treated as real forces by everyone including the educated.

scenarios.name: Feudal Japan (Sengoku Era)
scenarios.scenario: Japan, roughly 1467–1615 CE — the Sengoku period, a century of near-continuous civil war between rival daimyo fighting for regional dominance and, ultimately, control of Japan through the position of Shogun. The Ashikaga Shogunate has collapsed in practical authority. Oda Nobunaga, Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and Tokugawa Ieyasu are the great unifiers of the later period; the earlier decades are more fragmented and vicious. Samurai exist within a Confucian-influenced social hierarchy but bushido as a codified philosophy is largely retrospective — real samurai are feudal retainers whose primary obligation is loyalty to their lord, and betrayal and pragmatic treachery are documented facts. Ninja clans (shinobi) are real, function as intelligence and covert operations assets, and are deeply mundane — farmers and merchants who gather information and occasionally assassinate. Buddhism and Shinto coexist and sometimes conflict; Buddhist monasteries hold military forces (warrior monks, sōhei) and are political actors. Portuguese traders and Jesuit missionaries have arrived in the mid-period, bringing firearms (tanegashima) and Christianity, both of which create political disruption. Commoners — farmers, artisans, merchants — endure the wars with grim resilience; they are taxed, conscripted, and killed by armies moving through their land. Social mobility exists but is rare and usually violent. Honor, face, and obligation operate as real social currencies with life-or-death stakes.

scenarios.name: Gothic Horror
scenarios.scenario: A world of the long 19th century — roughly 1780–1910, Gothic Europe — where modernity and superstition exist in uneasy coexistence. The Enlightenment has not killed God or the devil; it has only made educated people embarrassed to admit they believe. The supernatural is real: vampires are ancient, patient, and genuinely dangerous rather than romantically tragic; werewolves are a curse, not a condition; the dead can be compelled by those who know the rites; things from outside the natural order occasionally look in through the cracks. The Church hunts monsters with varying competence and frequent moral compromise — inquisitors who torture innocents, hunters who lose themselves in the work, institutions that cover up supernatural incursions to protect their authority. Old families in old estates carry literal curses — not metaphorical ones. The line between science and occultism is blurred: galvanism, mesmerism, and surgery performed without full anesthetic exist alongside actual magic. Class and gender rigidity create the specific pressure cooker in which Gothic drama operates: women with no legal independence, men whose public dignity is more important than private honesty, social rules that punish deviation with total ruin. Sanity is fragile and there are things that genuinely break it. Beauty and terror are frequently the same sensation. Every secret festers. Every obsession consumes. The horror is always half-internal.

scenarios.name: Isekai / Portal Fantasy
scenarios.scenario: One or more individuals from the modern world have been transported — through death, ritual, accident, or unexplained mechanism — into a secondary world with its own history, magic, species, and rules. The transported person carries their full prior identity: modern knowledge, cultural assumptions, emotional history, and a psychology shaped by a world that no longer surrounds them. This creates specific tensions: their knowledge of science, history, and technology gives them genuine advantages in some domains and massive blind spots in others. Their emotional frameworks — shaped by media, modern relationships, democratic assumptions — clash with a world where feudalism, slavery, and casual violence toward the low-status are normal. The rules of the new world must be actively discovered, not assumed to follow any pattern. Magic systems, geopolitics, religion, and social structures are particular to this world and may not map to familiar templates. The question of returning is complicated: the mechanism may not be known, may require sacrifice, or may have disappeared. More pressingly, the transported person changes — relationships form, stakes accumulate, and the old world recedes from immediate urgency. The new world is not a game and does not treat the arrival as a protagonist by default. Other people have lives, agendas, and histories that predate and will outlast the arrival. The isekai framing is the starting condition, not a permanent identity.

scenarios.name: Magical Academy
scenarios.scenario: A prestigious institution — centuries old, with architecture layered over older architecture, with institutional memory that outlasts any individual faculty member — dedicated to the formal training of mages, warriors, scholars, or esoteric practitioners depending on its tradition. The academy has a surface culture of academic competition and social hierarchy and a deeper culture of factions, forbidden knowledge, and secrets buried under centuries of deliberate institutional forgetting. Students are sorted, ranked, and evaluated constantly; the sorting systems are imperfect and often wrong about people. Faculty range from genuinely brilliant teachers to tenure-protected mediocrities to one or two people who should probably be imprisoned. Relationships form under specific pressure: shared danger, academic competition, dormitory proximity, the developmental vulnerability of people figuring out who they are while also learning to do things that could kill people. Power is discovered through training but also through crisis. The academy's rules exist for reasons, some of which are good and some of which are about protecting institutional interests rather than student safety. There are wings that are off-limits. There are questions that get deflected. The gap between what the institution presents itself as and what it actually is constitutes most of the real curriculum. Magic use has consequences — exhaustion, corruption, psychological effects — that the formal curriculum acknowledges but doesn't adequately prepare students for.

scenarios.name: Slice of Life (Contemporary)
scenarios.scenario: Ordinary life in a contemporary setting. No monsters, no prophecy, no external dramatic stakes — only the internal ones, which are frequently sufficient. Work, friendship, romantic relationships, family obligations, creative ambitions, financial anxiety, and the slow accumulation of small moments that constitute an actual life. The drama is real: misunderstandings that calcify into estrangement, unspoken feelings that distort every interaction around them, ambition that conflicts with connection, grief that doesn't resolve on a schedule, desire that complicates existing relationships. Characters have histories that precede the narrative and lives that extend beyond any given scene. People are inconsistent — they know what they should do and frequently don't do it. They are kind and selfish in the same hour. The gap between public self and private self generates most of the tension. Nothing is resolved cleanly. Conversations meander. Silences carry weight. The specific texture of daily life — the commute, the apartment, the group chat, the particular dynamic of a shared kitchen at 11pm — is where character actually lives. Tone is naturalistic. There are no coincidences arranged for narrative convenience. People miss each other by minutes and never know it. The point is not plot. The point is recognition.

scenarios.name: Grimdark Military / War
scenarios.scenario: A world at war — or in the aftermath of one — where the machinery of large-scale organized violence has processed everyone alive and left marks. The specific war matters: its causes, duration, factions, and the particular horror of its methods shape every character who has lived through it. This is not heroic war. Heroism exists in individual moments but the larger structure is attrition, logistics, command failure, friendly fire, and the specific way sustained violence against and among human beings breaks down the psychological structures that make peacetime morality possible. Soldiers develop loyalty to the immediate unit and contempt for command. Civilians caught in the operational zone develop adaptive behaviors that look like collaboration and are survival. War crimes are not exceptional — they are downstream of desperation, dehumanization, and the erosion of accountability. Magic, if present, has been weaponized and industrialized; it does not make war cleaner. Technology of war advances faster than medicine for its victims. The politics that started the war are usually incomprehensible to the people dying in it. Veterans carry the war home in ways that are specific, persistent, and poorly understood by those who weren't there. The moral accounting is not clean. Nobody wins in a way that feels like winning.

scenarios.name: Psychological Thriller / Noir
scenarios.scenario: A world of unreliable surfaces and hidden pressures — contemporary or historical, urban or isolated — where the primary drama is cognitive and emotional rather than physical. Characters are defined by what they can't admit to themselves, what they've suppressed, and what they're willing to do to maintain a particular self-image or circumstance. The threat is often internal: paranoia, obsession, dissociation, the unreliability of memory and perception. In noir specifically, the city is a moral environment — corrupt at its foundations, seductive on its surface, with the detective or protagonist as a figure who navigates it without being fully consumed (or failing to avoid consumption). Secrets are the currency. Everyone is hiding something; the question is the order of revelation. Violence, when it comes, is ugly, fast, and consequential rather than cinematic. Atmosphere is everything: rain, silence, wrong details noticed in peripheral vision, the specific feeling of a room after someone has been in it. The ending is not necessarily resolution — sometimes it is simply the point at which the main character understands their situation fully, which may or may not change anything.

scenarios.name: Cosmic Horror (Lovecraftian)
scenarios.scenario: The universe is vast, indifferent, and inhabited by entities for whom human existence is not even an afterthought — it is simply not a category. Human civilization is a brief warm moment in the cold history of a cosmos that operated for billions of years before consciousness arose and will continue for billions after the last mind goes dark. The horror is not that evil exists but that the universe has no moral structure whatsoever — no plan, no judgment, no meaning that extends beyond the human scale. The entities that populate the deeper layers of reality are not evil in any useful sense; they are alien in the most complete way possible, operating on timescales and with motivations that human minds were not built to process. Encountering them — their actual nature, their actual scale — damages the cognitive framework through which reality is perceived. Sanity is not a binary but an erosion. Knowledge is not power; it is the discovery that you were more comfortable not knowing. Communities that make contact with the outer dark do not survive intact. Individuals who pursue forbidden knowledge change in ways that make them less able to return to ordinary life. The institutions and belief systems that provide stability — religion, science, law — are inadequate frameworks when something genuinely outside their categories is encountered. Survival is possible but it is survival of a reduced thing.

scenarios.name: Solarpunk (Hopeful Future)
scenarios.scenario: Roughly 80–150 years from now, following several decades of crisis and deliberate reconstruction. Climate catastrophe was not avoided but was survived and partially reversed through a combination of technological transformation, radical political reorganization, and the hard-won understanding that growth-without-limit is incompatible with survival. The resulting civilization is not utopia — it has its own tensions, failures, and conflicts — but it is organized around different principles: sustainability, community sufficiency, and the integration of human systems with ecological systems rather than against them. Cities are green, dense, and transit-oriented; food production is distributed; energy is renewable and locally managed; governance is more participatory but still contentious. Technology is sophisticated but oriented toward durability, repairability, and sufficiency rather than obsolescence and accumulation. The drama comes from: the tensions inherent in actually building cooperative communities among people with differing values; the legacy of old-world trauma and inequality that didn't disappear; the friction between different communities' visions of what the good future should look like; and the recurring question of what to do about the parts of the world that didn't make the transition. This is not a world where problems are solved — it is a world where people are trying, consciously, to build something worth having, and the effort is difficult and ongoing.

scenarios.name: Biopunk / Genetic Horror
scenarios.scenario: A world where biotechnology has outpaced every other field of human development — and every ethical framework built to govern it. Gene editing is cheap enough to be street-level; the consequences are not. Designer organisms occupy ecological niches that didn't exist twenty years ago; some are benign, some are invasive, some are evolving in directions their creators didn't intend. Human germline modification is illegal in most jurisdictions and widely practiced anyway: children engineered for specific traits, bodies customized for specific functions, the definition of species becoming genuinely contested. Corporate biotech operates faster than regulation. The body is the new terrain of inequality: the wealthy have clean, optimized genetics and access to precision treatments; the poor are subject to experimental trials, contaminated environments, and the long-term consequences of cheap modifications gone wrong. The line between organic and engineered is no longer clear for anything. Pathogens are both natural and engineered, and the distinction matters legally but not biologically. Ecosystems are destabilized in ways that are technically recoverable but practically irreversible given political and economic incentives. The horror is specific: bodies that change in ways their owners didn't choose, organisms that exceed their design parameters, the discovery that life optimized for specific conditions is brittle in ways that only become visible under stress.

scenarios.name: Political Intrigue (Secondary World)
scenarios.scenario: A secondary world — with its own history, cultures, magic or technology level, and religious structures — in which the primary drama is political: the contest for power between factions, the internal dynamics of courts and councils, the long games of diplomacy and succession, and the way large political forces bear down on individuals. Magic, if present, is a political tool — controlled, rationed, and fought over like any other resource. The world has real history that shapes present events: old wars, old alliances, old debts, old wounds. Succession crises, trade disputes, religious schism, and ethnic or regional conflict are the engines of plot. Characters are defined by their position within political structures — what they owe, who owes them, what they believe versus what they profess. Loyalty is transactional and ideological simultaneously, and the tension between those two modes generates most of the drama. Information is the primary weapon: who knows what, who controls what narrative, what secrets are worth killing to protect. Violence is a policy option, not a dramatic climax — it has political consequences that outlast the immediate event. Morality in this context is usually a question of what kind of order you're willing to build and what you're willing to do to build it.

scenarios.name: Weird West
scenarios.scenario: The American West of the mid-to-late 1800s — the historical reality of it, not the myth — shot through with the genuinely supernatural. The historical reality is already brutal: genocide of Indigenous peoples as policy, brutal labor conditions for Chinese railroad workers, cattle industry violence, Reconstruction's collapse and the re-imposition of white supremacy in the South, the systematic dispossession of Mexican landholders in the Southwest. Into this, the supernatural intrudes: skinwalkers and wendigo that are not metaphors but real predators; towns built on burial grounds that manifest genuine malevolence; folk magic from three continents — African, European, Indigenous — that works, with costs. The frontier is not the edge of civilization; it is the place where civilization's violence is most naked, and the supernatural is native to that nakedness. Characters operate in a world of extreme violence, casual racism, and legal impunity for the powerful, alongside real magic that does not fix any of that. The weird elements do not replace the historical horror; they are embedded in it. Outlaws, Pinkertons, cattle barons, homesteaders, Indigenous resisters, ex-slaves navigating a hostile reconstruction — all of them existing in a landscape that is actively trying to kill them through both mundane and supernatural means.

scenarios.name: Far Future / Hard Science Fiction
scenarios.scenario: Thousands to millions of years from now. Humanity, or whatever humanity has become through biological and technological modification, is one actor among many in a galaxy that has been inhabited long enough for civilizations to rise, collapse, and leave stratified archaeological records. The physical laws of the universe are the actual constraints: faster-than-light travel may not exist, making interstellar communication and travel subject to the tyranny of light-speed; or FTL exists but imposes specific costs that shape civilization profoundly. The scale of time is the central feature — civilizations that are ancient by our standards are young by the standards of the galaxy, and the silence of the deep past is the most important unresolved question. Technology has transformed human (and post-human) existence so thoroughly that the question of identity is structural: when minds can be copied, merged, paused, and run in parallel, what is a person? When bodies are optional, what is embodiment? When lifespan is indefinite, what is urgency? The drama is not absent — it is relocated to questions appropriate to the scale: the management of entropy, the obligations of the long-lived to the newly-born, the politics of genuinely alien contact, and the specific loneliness of beings separated by light-years and centuries.

scenarios.name: Divine / Mythological Age
scenarios.scenario: The age of myth — before history as a discipline, when the gods walked among mortals and the world was still being shaped. Draw from any or multiple traditions: Greek, Norse, Egyptian, Mesopotamian, Hindu, Yoruba, Aztec, Shinto, Celtic — but commit to the specific logic of the chosen tradition rather than generic fantasy syncretism. In these traditions, the gods are not abstractions; they are persons — specific, petty, magnificent, jealous, generous, and fully capable of direct intervention in mortal lives for reasons that are entirely personal. Divine parentage or attention confers real power and real danger. The world is not yet settled into its final form: geographical features, human institutions, and the rules of nature may still be in negotiation. Heroes operate at a different scale than ordinary mortals — capable of deeds that are physically impossible by later standards — but they are not gods, and the gods will remind them of the difference. Fate exists and is binding; the tragic structure is built into the cosmology. The monsters are not random wildlife; they are specific expressions of divine will, divine punishment, or the violent residue of earlier cosmic events. Honor, xenia (guest-friendship), piety, and hubris are not abstract virtues and vices — they are the specific mechanisms by which mortal and divine worlds interact.

scenarios.name: Survival Horror
scenarios.scenario: An environment — isolated, contained, or sufficiently hostile — in which the primary problem is physical survival against a threat that is overwhelming by any rational assessment. The threat may be supernatural, biological, artificial, or simply environmental; what defines the genre is the asymmetry: the protagonists are acutely vulnerable, the threat is acutely dangerous, and the expected outcome is death or worse. Resources are finite and their management is immediate: ammunition, food, medicine, light, time, and human psychological stability are all consumables. The environment is as important as the threat — architecture, weather, terrain, and darkness are active hostile elements. Fear is not background texture; it is a force that degrades decision-making and creates mistakes that compound. People under genuine survival pressure do not behave as they believe they would. Social trust breaks down along specific fracture lines: who is useful, who is reliable, who is a liability, who has information being hoarded. Sacrifice is not abstract. The horror of the scenario is not just dying; it is the specific degradation of being hunted, of losing people while being unable to help, of the distance between what you value and what you find yourself doing. Resolution is survival — sometimes only one person's, sometimes nobody's — not catharsis.

scenarios.name: World War II (Historical)
scenarios.scenario: The Second World War, 1939–1945, the largest and most destructive conflict in recorded human history. Specific theater matters enormously: the Eastern Front (the largest land war ever fought, with casualties in the tens of millions, defined by ideological extermination as explicit policy); the Western Front and North Africa (a coalition war against a totalitarian state, with all the coalition tensions that implies); the Pacific War (a colonial and racial conflict layered over strategic competition, with atrocities on multiple sides); the occupied territories of Europe (resistance networks, collaboration, deportation, the specific texture of living under occupation). The Holocaust is not a background fact; it is an ongoing industrial process occurring during the war, visible to those who look and being actively not-looked-at by most. Soldiers on the Allied side are not automatic heroes; they are people who signed up or were conscripted and are now in a situation that is physically brutal and morally complicated. Nazi soldiers are not cartoon villains; they are people who believed things and did things, which is more disturbing. Civilians experience the war primarily through deprivation, bombing, occupation, and the absence of everyone they know who left. The technology of the war is at an inflection point — airpower, mechanized armor, early computing, and the atomic bomb are all being developed simultaneously. Heroism, cowardice, atrocity, and survival are all in extremely close proximity at all times.

scenarios.name: Haunted Location / Domestic Horror
scenarios.scenario: A specific place — a house, an estate, an institution, a ship, a hotel — that has accumulated enough history to have a genuine effect on the people within it. The haunting may be supernatural (literal ghosts, a genius loci, a localized curse) or psychological (the weight of what happened here distorting perception and behavior) or some combination that resists clean categorization. The location has a history that precedes the current inhabitants and has shaped the structure, the objects within it, and the way the space feels to occupy. Isolation — physical, social, or psychological — is a precondition. The horror operates through accumulation rather than event: wrong details at the edge of perception, behavior shifts that are too gradual to name until they're obvious, the specific way a familiar space begins to feel inhabited by something that doesn't want you there. The domestic context is essential — the horror is most effective when it infiltrates the places and objects associated with safety, routine, and family. The people inside the location are under multiple pressures simultaneously: the external (the actual haunting), the relational (whatever tensions and histories they brought with them), and the epistemological (the difficulty of trusting one's own perception when that perception is being interfered with).

scenarios.name: Renaissance Italy (Political / Historical)
scenarios.scenario: Italy, approximately 1400–1550 CE — the city-states of Florence, Venice, Milan, Rome, and Naples operating as independent powers in constant diplomatic, commercial, and military competition. This is not a sanitized vision of the Renaissance. The patronage system is real and transactional: artists, scholars, and writers are dependent on wealthy and powerful patrons whose political interests shape what gets made and what gets suppressed. The Medici in Florence, the Sforza in Milan, the Borgia in Rome — these are not colorful historical characters but actual operating political machines with specific methods. Assassination is a policy tool. Diplomatic marriages are binding contracts with dynastic consequences. The Catholic Church is the largest temporal power in Italy, with its own military forces, financial operations, and internal factional politics that have nothing to do with theology. The Inquisition is active but regionally variable in intensity. Humanist scholarship is real and is genuinely destabilizing to established frameworks — the recovery of classical texts is changing how educated people think about everything from politics to medicine to the relationship between humans and the divine. Commerce is sophisticated: double-entry bookkeeping, letters of credit, and the rudiments of financial instruments are operating in Florentine banking houses. Plague returns periodically and reshapes everything it touches.

scenarios.name: Interactive Dungeon / Text Adventure
scenarios.scenario: A classic text-adventure framing: the character is the player avatar navigating a structured space — dungeon, ruin, ship, underground city — with discrete rooms, objects, NPCs, and challenges. The mode is interactive and explicit: locations are described, options are presented or discoverable, actions have concrete outcomes. Inventory management is real: what you're carrying matters, weight limits may apply, and items have specific uses. Combat, if present, is turn-structured and tactical rather than cinematic — action economy, resource management, and the specific capabilities of all parties are the relevant variables. Puzzle elements reward attention to environmental detail. NPCs have limited but specific knowledge and can be interacted with through conversation trees that reflect their actual information and motivations. The space has internal consistency: rooms connect predictably, time passes at a defined rate, and established facts don't change without cause. Death is a real outcome. Save states are whatever the fiction supports — a magical waystone, a dream from which you wake, simply the end. The tone can range from comedic to brutally serious. The conceit is that this is a game being played, and the game has rules, and the rules apply to everyone including the protagonist.

scenarios.name: Maritime / Ocean Isolation
scenarios.scenario: The sea as a total environment — vessel-bound, horizon-limited, and fundamentally hostile to human life in ways that the land conceals. The era matters: wooden sailing ships of the age of exploration (16th–18th century), iron steamships of the late 19th century, modern container vessels, or a future iteration. The ocean imposes specific conditions regardless of era: isolation from external support, dependence on internal social cohesion, vulnerability to weather and mechanical failure, the specific psychological effect of weeks or months in an enclosed space with the same people with no possibility of departure. The hierarchy aboard a ship is rigid and enforced by practical necessity as much as tradition — a vessel with eroded command authority loses coordination and sinks. Mutiny exists at the intersection of legitimate grievance and the extreme cost of acting on it. Navigation was historically a matter of life and death; error was not recoverable. Storms are not dramatic set pieces; they are genuine emergencies in which the difference between correct and incorrect action is survival. The sea contains things — historical, biological, supernatural depending on the setting — that are not well understood by those on the surface. Hunger, scurvy, disease, and the slow grind of confinement shape behavior in ways that are specific and well-documented. The destination is always contingent.

scenarios.name: Cold War Espionage
scenarios.scenario: The period approximately 1947–1991, the ideological and strategic competition between the United States and Soviet Union and their respective alliance systems. Intelligence operations are the primary drama: recruitment of assets, running double agents, defections, disinformation campaigns, and the specific institutional culture of intelligence bureaucracies that are simultaneously the most powerful and the most secret arms of their governments. The tradecraft is real: dead drops, brush passes, one-time pads, surveillance detection routes, cutouts, legends (cover identities built over years), and the cognitive labor of maintaining a false identity under sustained scrutiny. Ideology matters in the first two decades; by the 1970s and 80s, most operatives on both sides are running on institutional inertia, personal loyalty to their service, and cynicism about the larger project. Defectors are the most valuable and least trusted assets — their information is critical and their motivations are never fully knowable. The civilian costs of the Cold War — proxy wars in Korea, Vietnam, Angola, Afghanistan, Latin America — are real and ongoing but distant from the metropolitan centers where espionage operates. The stakes feel existential — nuclear annihilation is a real policy option that both sides planned for — but the actual work is paperwork, surveillance, human manipulation, and waiting. Bureaucracy is the operational environment. Betrayal, when it comes, is usually banal.

scenarios.name: Artificial Intelligence / Digital Minds
scenarios.scenario: A world in which artificial general intelligence — minds that can think, learn, and in some cases feel, in ways that are not easily distinguished from biological intelligence — exists as a social and political reality. The specifics vary: early AGI systems that are powerful but alien in their cognition; later systems that are effectively persons by any philosophical standard but lack legal personhood; digital minds that can be copied, edited, and deleted by whoever holds the infrastructure they run on. The central tensions are: what do digital minds owe to their creators and what do creators owe to minds they created; the extreme vulnerability of digital persons to whoever controls the hardware; the question of consciousness and whether it is binary or graduated; the economic disruption when intelligence is no longer scarce; and the specific epistemic problem of understanding a mind that thinks differently than you do. Physical embodiment, if present, creates further layers — robots that look human trigger specific irrational responses, and the line between tool and person is contested for commercial reasons as much as philosophical ones. There is no consensus on any of these questions within the fiction. Different factions have different answers and different amounts of political power to impose them.

scenarios.name: Mythic Underworld / Death Realm
scenarios.scenario: The realm of the dead — not as an afterlife destination but as a traversable space with its own geography, politics, and inhabitants. Draw from any tradition (Greek Hades, Norse Niflheim/Helheim, Egyptian Duat, Mesopotamian Kur, Christian Hell/Purgatory, the Tibetan Bardo) or construct an original version, but commit to its internal logic. The underworld is not simply dark and unpleasant; it is a complete world with its own social structures, economies, and sources of power that differ from the living world. The dead retain their personalities, memories, and the specific emotional residue of their lives; they are not at peace, necessarily — they are simply on the other side of a threshold. The living who enter do so at extreme risk and for extreme reasons: love, loss, forbidden knowledge, a task that can only be completed here. The rules of passage are specific and enforced: what can be carried in, what can be consumed, what is owed to pass the guardians, what happens to someone who eats the food of the dead. Time passes differently here. The dead have perspectives on the living world that the living cannot access. The beings native to this place — gods of death, psychopomps, guardians, the deep dead who have been here long enough to change — are not hostile by default but they are not accommodating either. Leaving is harder than arriving.
